one_line_takeaway: Fine-tuning small open LLMs (Llama3-8B/Qwen2-7B) with FFT plus Random Over Sampling on a new 4,998-contract real-world DApp dataset lifts smart-contract vulnerability detection from F1=0.20 (prompting) to F1=0.83 and excels at hard price-manipulation logical bugs, but the unpolished manuscript and thin evaluation protocol temper confidence.
key_finding: Fine-tuning transforms a near-useless prompt-only LLM into a competitive detector: Qwen2-7B by prompting alone scores F1=0.20 (PRE 0.14, REC 0.37), whereas Llama3-8B-FFT reaches F1=0.73 (PRE 0.78, REC 0.70), and adding ROS augmentation raises Llama3-8B-FFT to F1=0.83 (PRE 0.84, REC 0.79) — the paper's best result. FFT beats LoRA throughout. On the hard price-manipulation (PMV) logical-error class the models are notably strong (Llama3-8B-FFT PRE 0.97 / REC 0.68; Qwen2-7B-FFT PRE 1.00 / REC 0.63), supporting the claim that fine-tuned LLMs handle machine-unauditable flaws. On precision the FFT models (0.78) beat the cited GPTLENS (0.64) and GPTSCAN (0.57).
core_contribution: The paper fine-tunes open-source LLMs (Llama3-8B, Qwen2-7B) to detect vulnerabilities in real-world DApp smart contracts. Its two concrete own contributions are: (1) a new dataset of 215 real-world DApp projects comprising 4,998 Solidity contracts, assembled with a custom Python tool 'SmartCollect' that resolves import dependencies (public libraries, external/OpenZeppelin contracts) to reconstruct complete multi-contract projects, plus 23 contracts carrying token price-manipulation (logical) vulnerabilities; and (2) an empirical demonstration that domain-specific fine-tuning (Full-Parameter Fine-Tuning and LoRA), combined with Random Over Sampling (ROS) to fix class imbalance, substantially outperforms prompt-only LLMs and two prior GPT-based tools, and is especially strong on non-machine-auditable price-manipulation flaws. Note: much of the discussion of COBRA, SCALM, StateGuard, GasTrace, etc. is cited related work, not this paper's own method.
threat_model: Adversaries exploit vulnerabilities in the immutable smart contracts underlying DApps to steal digital assets; the paper cites SlowMist's figure of 466 blockchain hacking incidents in 2023 causing ~$2.49B in losses. The defender is an auditor/developer trying to identify vulnerabilities before deployment. The paper targets both conventional machine-auditable bugs (reentrancy, arithmetic/integer overflow, timestamp dependence) and, critically, 'machine-unauditable' logical errors like token price manipulation, which it says account for nearly 80% of contract vulnerabilities and evade pattern-based static analysis. The detector is a defensive tool, not an attack.
limitations: The manuscript is visibly unpolished/likely not peer-reviewed: it retains ACM template placeholders ('Do Not Use This Code -> Generate the Correct Terms', '2018', 'this chapter'), so treat claims cautiously. Methodological gaps: no train/test split, cross-validation, or data-leakage controls are described, and ROS duplication risks the same contract appearing in train and test, potentially inflating scores. The SOTA comparison (Table 3) reports only precision, omitting recall/F1, which favors the proposed method and is not apples-to-apples. The PMV logical-error evaluation rests on just 23 contracts, so its high precision has wide uncertainty. Timestamp-dependence precision is weak (0.44-0.47 across models), and Qwen2-7B-FFT shows an extreme arithmetic-vuln tradeoff (PRE 1.00 but REC 0.19). No dataset/code release, hyperparameters, or hardware are reported, hurting reproducibility.

datasets_used: Author-collected dataset: 215 real-world DApp projects / 4,998 Solidity smart contracts, gathered from Code4rena and Slowmist via the custom SmartCollect dependency-resolution tool
23 contracts with token price-manipulation (PMV) logical-error vulnerabilities (subset for RQ3)
Vulnerability categories targeted: reentrancy (RV), arithmetic (AV), timestamp dependence (TDV), price manipulation (PMV); vulnerability taxonomy per the SWC Registry
quantitative_results: Qwen2-7B prompt-only (no fine-tuning): PRE 0.14, REC 0.37, F1 0.20 (Table 2)
Llama3-8B-FFT-ROS: PRE 0.84, REC 0.79, F1 0.83 (Table 5; best overall)
ROS augmentation improved precision by 6-7% and recall by 9-16% vs non-augmented (Tables 1 vs 5)
Price manipulation (PMV) detection: Llama3-8B-FFT PRE 0.97 / REC 0.68; Qwen2-7B-FFT PRE 1.00 / REC 0.63; Qwen2-7B-LoRA PRE 0.97 / REC 0.63 (Table 4)
Per-type precision/recall (Table 4, Llama3-8B-FFT): RV 0.82/0.63, AV 0.63/0.70, TDV 0.47/0.98, PMV 0.97/0.68

Context stats cited from prior work: prompt-engineered GPT ~40% accuracy, RAG-enhanced ~60%; ~80% of contract vulnerabilities are machine-unauditable; SlowMist reports 466 hacks / ~$2.49B losses in 2023
baselines_compared: Internal comparison of FFT vs LoRA fine-tuning, and with vs without ROS augmentation
